id: quiz - Chapter 03 - Reporting Information through Financial Statements
title: Chapter 3 - Reporting Information through Financial Statements
description: Quiz on the principles and standards of financial reporting, including the components of financial statements, interim reporting, and accounting for changes and errors, based on the provided Chapter 3 documents.
subject: ACC1370 - Financial Accounting and Reporting

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a purpose of general-purpose financial statements, according to the provided material?
options: To provide information useful to a wide range of users in making economic decisions.
To provide reports tailored to the particular information needs of a specific group of users.
To show the result of management's stewardship of the resources entrusted to the entity.
To assist users in predicting the entity's future cash flows.
questions.answer: To provide reports tailored to the particular information needs of a specific group of users.
questions.rationale: General-purpose financial statements are intended to meet the needs of users who are not in a position to require an entity to prepare reports tailored to their particular information needs[cite: 8].

questions.question: LKAS 1 will be replaced by which standard with effect from January 1, 2027?
options: LKAS 34
LKAS 8
SLFRS 9
SLFRS 18
questions.answer: SLFRS 18
questions.rationale: The document states that LKAS 1 will be replaced by SLFRS 18 with effect from 01 January 2027[cite: 7].

questions.question: An entity applies an accounting policy retrospectively. What additional financial statement must be included in the complete set of financial statements?
options: An additional Statement of Changes in Equity.
A statement of financial position at the beginning of the earliest comparative period.
A condensed Statement of Financial Position.
A separate Statement of Other Comprehensive Income.
questions.answer: A statement of financial position at the beginning of the earliest comparative period.
questions.rationale: A complete set of financial statements includes 'A statement of financial position at the beginning of the earliest comparative period when an entity applies an accounting policy retrospectively or makes a retrospective restatement of items in its financial statements'[cite: 15].

questions.question: According to the general requirements of LKAS 1, what is the presumption regarding the reporting period for financial statements?
options: They must be prepared at least quarterly.
They can be prepared for any period as long as it is disclosed.
They will be prepared at least annually.
They must be prepared every six months.
questions.answer: They will be prepared at least annually.
questions.rationale: The document states, 'There is a presumption that financial statements will be prepared at least annually'[cite: 19].

questions.question: Which of the following would be an appropriate reason for an entity to NOT present a classified statement of financial position?
options: If the entity is in a service-based industry.
If the presentation based on liquidity provides better information.
If the entity is a public company.
If the entity is required to do so by local regulations.
questions.answer: If the presentation based on liquidity provides better information.
questions.rationale: An entity must normally present a classified statement of financial position... unless presentation based on liquidity presents better (i.e., reliable and more relevant) information[cite: 23].

questions.question: What is the primary content of the Statement of Profit or Loss and Other Comprehensive Income (SOPLOCI)?
options: Assets, liabilities, and equity.
Cash inflows and outflows from operating, investing, and financing activities.
Income and expenses for a specific period.
Changes in equity during a period.
questions.answer: Income and expenses for a specific period.
questions.rationale: The SOPLOCI 'Shows the Financial Performance for a period' and 'Consist of Income and Expenses'[cite: 42].

questions.question: What is the term for the 'change in equity during a period resulting from transactions and other events, other than those changes resulting from transactions with owners in their capacity as owners'?
options: Profit for the period
Retained earnings
Total comprehensive income
Net income
questions.answer: Total comprehensive income
questions.rationale: The document defines Total Comprehensive Income as 'The change in equity during a period resulting from transactions and other events, other than those changes resulting from transactions with owners in their capacity as owners'[cite: 49].

questions.question: Under which method of expense classification would 'Distribution costs' and 'Administration expenses' be presented?
options: Classification based on nature
Classification based on function
Classification based on materiality
Classification based on liquidity
questions.answer: Classification based on function
questions.rationale: The document shows an example of expense classification based on function that includes 'Distribution costs' and 'Administration expenses'[cite: 37, 38].

questions.question: Which of the following is an item that would be recognized in Other Comprehensive Income (OCI)?
options: Revenue from the sale of goods
Cost of sales
Gains and losses on remeasuring financial assets at fair value through OCI
Interest expense
questions.answer: Gains and losses on remeasuring financial assets at fair value through OCI
questions.rationale: The document lists 'Gains and losses on remeasuring financial assets at fair value through OCI in accordance with SLFRS 9' as an example of Other Comprehensive Income[cite: 52].

questions.question: Which financial statement is explicitly prepared on a cash basis?
options: Statement of Financial Position
Statement of Profit or Loss and Other Comprehensive Income
Statement of Changes in Equity
Statement of Cash Flows
questions.answer: Statement of Cash Flows
questions.rationale: The Statement of Cash Flows shows 'the inflows and outflows of cash and cash equivalents...' [cite: 54] and is the 'Only the financial statement is prepared on a cash basis'[cite: 55].

questions.question: What is the primary role of the Notes to the financial statements?
options: To replace the main financial statements if they are condensed.
To provide a summary of the financial performance.
To disclose additional information not presented elsewhere and explain the basis of preparation and policies used.
To provide an independent opinion on the financial statements.
questions.answer: To disclose additional information not presented elsewhere and explain the basis of preparation and policies used.
questions.rationale: The notes must: 'present information about the basis of preparation of the financial statements and the specific accounting policies used' and 'provide additional information that is not presented elsewhere in the financial statements but is relevant to an understanding of any of them'[cite: 59].

questions.question: Interim financial reporting is defined as the preparation and presentation of financial statements for what period?
options: A full financial year.
A period that is less than a full financial year.
A period of exactly three months.
A period of exactly six months.
questions.answer: A period that is less than a full financial year.
questions.rationale: Interim Financial Reporting (LKAS 34) is the 'Preparation and presentation of the Financial Statements for a period that is less than a full financial year'[cite: 334].

questions.question: What is the minimum requirement for a set of condensed interim financial statements?
options: They must be a complete set of financial statements.
They must only include the Statement of Financial Position and Statement of Profit or Loss.
They must include, at a minimum, each of the headings and sub-totals from the most recent annual statements.
They must be audited before presentation.
questions.answer: They must include, at a minimum, each of the headings and sub-totals from the most recent annual statements.
questions.rationale: If the financial statements are condensed, they should include, at a minimum, each of the headings and sub-totals included in the most recent annual financial statements[cite: 335].

questions.question: How should a change in an accounting estimate be recognized?
options: Retrospectively, by restating prior periods' financial statements.
By including it as an adjustment to retained earnings.
Prospectively, by including it in profit or loss in the current or future periods.
By making a direct adjustment to the Statement of Changes in Equity.
questions.answer: Prospectively, by including it in profit or loss in the current or future periods.
questions.rationale: The effect of a change in accounting estimate should be recognized prospectively by including it in profit or loss in the period of the change[cite: 361].

questions.question: According to LKAS 8, what are 'accounting policies'?
options: The monetary amounts in financial statements that are subject to measurement uncertainty.
Adjustments of the carrying amount of an asset or a liability.
The specific principles, bases, conventions, rules, and practices applied by an entity in preparing and presenting financial statements.
The corrections of errors in prior period financial statements.
questions.answer: The specific principles, bases, conventions, rules, and practices applied by an entity in preparing and presenting financial statements.
questions.rationale: Accounting policies are the specific principles, bases, conventions, rules, and practices applied by an entity in preparing and presenting financial statements[cite: 340].

questions.question: When can a change in accounting policy be made, as per the provided material?
options: When it is required by an SLFRS.
When it is changed to reflect accurately the usage and flow of inventories.
When it is changed from a function-based approach to a nature-based approach.

An entity can change an accounting policy when it leads to more reliable and more relevant information about the effects of transactions on the entity's financial position, performance, or cash flows.
questions.answer: An entity can change an accounting policy when it leads to more reliable and more relevant information about the effects of transactions on the entity's financial position, performance, or cash flows.
questions.rationale: The document shows a slide titled 'An entity can change an accounting policy in the following situations' and a box that says 'The change results in the financial statements providing more reliable and relevant information'[cite: 349].

questions.question: If a company changes its expense classification from a function-based approach to a nature-based approach, this is an example of a change in which aspect of financial reporting?
options: Recognition
Measurement Basis
Presentation
Accounting Estimate
questions.answer: Presentation
questions.rationale: The document lists 'Change in...Presentation – Change in expense classification from a function-based approach to a nature-based approach' as an example[cite: 348].

questions.question: According to the provided material, when is an entity considered to be 'impracticable' to apply a requirement?
options: When the entity cannot apply it after making every reasonable effort to do so.
When it is too expensive to apply.
When the application would lead to a material change in profit or loss.
When the change is not required by an SLFRS.
questions.answer: When the entity cannot apply it after making every reasonable effort to do so.
questions.rationale: The document defines 'Impracticable' as 'Applying a requirement is impracticable when the entity cannot apply it after making every reasonable effort to do so'[cite: 350].

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a change in accounting estimate?
options: Changing the inventory valuation method from FIFO to WAC.
Changing the income recognition policy of a university.
Changing the useful life or residual value of Property, Plant, and Equipment.
Correcting a prior period error.
questions.answer: Changing the useful life or residual value of Property, Plant, and Equipment.
questions.rationale: The document states that 'Useful life, residual value of PPE, depreciation method, warranty provision, fair values, etc.' are examples of accounting estimates that may change[cite: 358, 359].

questions.question: What is the required accounting treatment for a prior period error, according to the document?
options: It should be recognized prospectively.
It should be corrected retrospectively by restating the comparative amounts.
It should be included as an expense in the current period.
It should be included as a change in accounting estimate.
questions.answer: It should be corrected retrospectively by restating the comparative amounts.
questions.rationale: The document shows that 'Prior period errors should be corrected retrospectively in the first set of financial statements authorized for issue after their discovery by restating the comparative amounts for the prior period(s) presented in which the error occurred'[cite: 365].

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of a complete set of financial statements as per the document?
options: Statement of Financial Position.
Statement of Directors' Responsibility.
Statement of Cash Flows.
Notes, comprising a summary of significant accounting policies.
questions.answer: Statement of Directors' Responsibility.
questions.rationale: The document lists the Statement of Directors' Responsibility and Independent Auditors' Report as 'Other Reports Presented Together with Financial Statements' [cite: 62], not as part of the complete set of financial statements[cite: 15].

questions.question: How does the new IFRS 18 standard aim to improve financial reporting, as stated in the document?
options: By replacing all other SLFRS standards.
By affecting how companies measure financial performance.
By requiring additional defined subtotals in the statement of profit or loss and requiring disclosures about MPMs.
By eliminating the need for interim financial reports.
questions.answer: By requiring additional defined subtotals in the statement of profit or loss and requiring disclosures about MPMs.
questions.rationale: IFRS 18 aims to improve financial reporting by: 'requiring additional defined subtotals in the statement of profit or loss; requiring disclosures about management-defined performance measures (MPMs); and adding new principles for grouping'[cite: 47]. The document also states that IFRS 18 will not affect how companies measure financial performance, but how they present and disclose it[cite: 45].

questions.question: Under which principle of LKAS 1 is it required that assets and liabilities, and income and expenses, should not be offset unless required or permitted by a SLFRS?
options: Fair presentation and compliance.
Accrual basis of accounting.
Materiality and Aggregation.
Offsetting.
questions.answer: Offsetting.
questions.rationale: The document lists 'Offsetting' as one of the 'General Requirements of LKAS 1' and provides a more detailed explanation in the following slide, stating that 'an entity shall not offset assets and liabilities or income and expenses, unless required or permitted by a SLFRS'[cite: 63, 64].

questions.question: If an entity changes its annual reporting period, what must it disclose in its financial statements?
options: The reason for the change and a statement that amounts are not entirely comparable.
A full set of condensed financial statements for the comparative period.
A restated Statement of Financial Position.
A note explaining the impact on the company's going concern.
questions.answer: The reason for the change and a statement that amounts are not entirely comparable.
questions.rationale: If the annual reporting period changes... the entity must disclose the reason for the change and state that amounts are not entirely comparable[cite: 20].

questions.question: What is the effect of the introduction of IFRS 18 on interim financial reporting (LKAS 34)?
options: It eliminates the need for interim reports.
It requires the disclosure of information about Management-defined Performance Measures (MPMs).
It makes interim reports mandatory for all entities.
It requires a full set of financial statements to be prepared for every interim period.
questions.answer: It requires the disclosure of information about Management-defined Performance Measures (MPMs).
questions.rationale: With the introduction of IFRS 18, a few amendments were made to LKAS 34. Those include: 'Disclose information about MPMs in interim financial statements'[cite: 337].

questions.question: According to the document, in the absence of a specific accounting standard, what is the correct descending order of sources management should refer to in selecting an accounting policy?
options: Conceptual Framework, pronouncements of other standard-setting bodies, and industry practices.
Pronouncements of other standard-setting bodies, Conceptual Framework, and the requirements in SLFRSs.
The requirements in SLFRSs dealing with similar issues, and the definitions and recognition criteria in the Conceptual Framework.
Industry practices, Conceptual Framework, and the requirements in SLFRSs.
questions.answer: The requirements in SLFRSs dealing with similar issues, and the definitions and recognition criteria in the Conceptual Framework.
questions.rationale: In the absence of a recommended accounting policy, management must refer to, and consider the applicability of, the following sources in descending order: 'the requirements in SLFRSs dealing with similar and related issues; and the definitions, recognition criteria and measurement concepts for assets, liabilities, income and expenses in the Conceptual Framework'[cite: 342, 343].

questions.question: What is the impact of a change in an accounting policy on comparative information if the change is applied retrospectively?
options: The comparative information is not affected.
The comparative information must be restated as if the new policy had always been applied.
The comparative information is only restated in the current period's notes.
The comparative information must be changed to the new policy's values without restating the original report.
questions.answer: The comparative information must be restated as if the new policy had always been applied.
questions.rationale: The document states that a change in accounting policy is a 'retrospective application' [cite: 350], and one of the components of a complete set of financial statements is 'Comparative information in respect of the preceding period (in above 1-5)' and 'A statement of financial position at the beginning of the earliest comparative period when an entity applies an accounting policy retrospectively or makes a retrospective restatement'[cite: 15]. The examples and activities demonstrate the retrospective restatement of prior period financial statements to reflect the new policy[cite: 351, 352, 353, 354, 355].

questions.question: What is the purpose of preparing general-purpose financial statements?
options: To provide a single comprehensive report for all users.
To fulfill the information requirements of a wide range of users who need information to make economic decisions.
To serve as the primary source for a company's tax filings.
To only provide information on financial performance.
questions.answer: To fulfill the information requirements of a wide range of users who need information to make economic decisions.
questions.rationale: The document states that general-purpose financial statements are prepared 'To fulfil the above information requirements of users' which include the need for information about financial position, performance, and cash flows to make economic decisions[cite: 5].

questions.question: Which of the following describes the content of a Statement of Financial Position (SOFP)?
options: It shows the financial performance for a period.
It presents the financial position of an entity at a specific point in time.
It shows the changes in equity during a period.
It details the cash inflows and outflows over a period.
questions.answer: It presents the financial position of an entity at a specific point in time.
questions.rationale: The Statement of Financial Position (SOFP) 'Shows the Financial Position of an entity as of the reporting date'[cite: 21]. It shows 'The resources and claims to resources as of the reporting date'[cite: 22].

questions.question: What is the primary difference between a change in accounting policy and a change in accounting estimate?
options: A change in policy is applied prospectively, while a change in estimate is applied retrospectively.
A change in policy corrects a past mistake, while a change in estimate reflects new information or experience.
A change in policy is applied retrospectively, while a change in estimate is applied prospectively.
A change in policy affects profit or loss, while a change in estimate only affects the balance sheet.
questions.answer: A change in policy is applied retrospectively, while a change in estimate is applied prospectively.
questions.rationale: Changes in accounting policies are generally applied retrospectively [cite: 350], while the effect of a change in an accounting estimate should be recognized prospectively[cite: 361]. A change in estimate is an 'adjustment of the carrying amount of an asset or a liability... which results from the assessment of the present status of, and expected future benefits and obligations associated with, assets and liabilities'[cite: 360].
